title: Having a permissive Cross-Origin Resource Sharing policy is security-sensitive
description: Having a permissive Cross-Origin Resource Sharing policy is security-sensitiveAnalyze your codeintentionality-completesecuritySecurity HotspotcweHaving a permissive Cross-Origin Resource Sharing policy is security-sensitive. It has led in the past to the following vulnerabilities:CVE-2018-0269CVE-2017-14460Same origin policyin browsers prevents, by default and for security-reasons, a javascript frontend to perform a cross-origin HTTP request to a resource that has a different origin (domain, protocol, or port) from its own. The requested target can append additional HTTP headers in response, calledCORS, that act like directives for the browser and change the access control policy / relax the same origin policy.Ask Yourself WhetherYou don’t trust the origin specified, example:Access-Control
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

logic.detection_conditions: Detect permissive CORS configurations: AllowAnyOrigin() method calls OR EnableCors attribute with wildcard (*)
logic.triggering_conditions: (Method call is AllowAnyOrigin) OR (Attribute is EnableCors with wildcard value) AND (NOT in excluded contexts)
logic.reasoning: This rule detects permissive CORS policies that allow any origin, which is a security risk. It targets two patterns: (1) AllowAnyOrigin() method calls in UseCors configuration, (2) [EnableCors("*")] attributes with wildcard. Excludes comments and logging to avoid false positives on documentation and examples. AST-first approach targeting CallExpression and Attribute nodes.
